title: Governance, Globalization & Contemporary Issues
simple_html:
This capstone lesson asks: how do we keep companies honest, and what new ethical problems arise when business goes global and digital?

Corporate governance is the system of rules and structures that direct and control a company — how it is held accountable to its owners and society. The core problem it solves is the agency problem: the people who run the company (managers) are not the people who own it (shareholders), so managers may pursue their own interests instead. Governance mechanisms — an independent board of directors, audits, executive pay tied to performance, and shareholder rights — exist to align those interests and prevent abuse.

Globalization creates a hard question: whose ethics apply when you operate in many countries? Relativism says "when in Rome, do as the Romans do." Universalism says some standards (like banning child labor) hold everywhere. Most thinkers land in between: respect local custom, but never violate basic human rights — and never confuse a bribe with a normal business practice.

Key takeaways

  • Governance exists to solve the agency problem (managers vs. owners).
  • Key tools: independent boards, audits, transparency, aligned incentives.
  • Global business forces a choice between relativism and universalism — aim for the middle.
  • Bribery, corruption, and supply-chain abuses are major global risks.
  • New frontiers: AI ethics, data privacy, and ESG accountability.
intermediate_html:
The final lesson integrates the course at the institutional and global level: the structures that hold corporations accountable (governance), the dilemmas of operating across cultures (globalization), and the emerging issues that will define business ethics going forward.

Corporate governance and the agency problem

Corporate governance is "the system by which companies are directed and controlled" (Cadbury Report, 1992). Its central justification is agency theory (Jensen & Meckling, 1976): because ownership (principals/shareholders) is separated from control (agents/managers), and the two have divergent interests and asymmetric information, managers may shirk, empire-build, or extract private benefits. Governance mechanisms reduce these agency costs.

Key mechanisms include: an independent board of directors that monitors management on shareholders' behalf (independence and separating the CEO and chair roles are central debates); audit committees and external auditors ensuring reliable financial reporting; executive compensation structured (e.g., equity, clawbacks) to align managers with long-term value; shareholder rights and activism; and the external market for corporate control (takeover threat). Post-Enron, the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (2002) tightened these requirements substantially.

Globalization: relativism vs. universalism

Operating across borders raises the question of which moral standards apply. Ethical relativism holds that morality is culture-bound, supporting the maxim "when in Rome." Ethical universalism holds that core moral standards (human dignity, basic rights) apply everywhere. Pure relativism is untenable — it would forbid criticizing any local practice, however harmful — yet naive universalism can become ethical imperialism.

Thomas Donaldson & Thomas Dunfee's (1999) Integrative Social Contracts Theory (ISCT) offers a principled middle path: certain hypernorms (universal principles like human-rights protections and anti-corruption) are non-negotiable, while within those bounds communities have "moral free space" to set their own norms. Donaldson's earlier test asks: would the practice be acceptable at home, and is it truly necessary to do business there?

Corruption and bribery

A recurring application is the distinction between a bribe (a payment to obtain improper advantage — corrupting a decision) and a facilitation payment (a small payment to speed a routine, lawful service). The US FCPA permits narrow facilitation payments; the UK Bribery Act (2010) bans them outright. Bribery distorts markets, harms development, and is a clear hypernorm violation under ISCT.

Contemporary frontier issues

IssueCore ethical tension
AI & data ethicsAlgorithmic bias, accountability, automation's impact on jobs.
PrivacyData collection and surveillance vs. individual rights.
Supply-chain ethicsCost and distance vs. labor rights and safety.
ESG & climateLong-term sustainability vs. short-term returns; greenwashing.

Worked mini-case

A firm's overseas agent requests "a fee to expedite customs." Analysis: Is it facilitation (small, routine, legal service) or a bribe (securing improper advantage)? Under the UK Bribery Act it is illegal regardless; under ISCT, anti-corruption is a hypernorm, so even if locally normalized, paying violates a non-negotiable standard. Decision: refuse, document, and address the structural pressure through compliance training and clear policy.

How to apply it

Strengthen governance (independent boards, robust audit, aligned pay, whistleblower channels); adopt a global code grounded in hypernorms while respecting legitimate local variation; conduct human-rights and ESG due diligence across the supply chain; and build ethics-by-design into data and AI systems.
full_html:
This capstone lesson scales the course's themes to their highest level — the institutions that hold corporations accountable and the dilemmas that arise when business crosses cultural, legal, and technological frontiers. It synthesizes governance theory, cross-cultural ethics, and the contemporary issues (AI, privacy, sustainability) that will define the field's next decade.

Corporate governance and the separation of ownership and control

The foundational diagnosis dates to Berle & Means (1932), who observed that in the modern public corporation ownership is dispersed among many shareholders while control rests with professional managers. Jensen & Meckling (1976) formalized this as the agency problem: the principal (shareholder) delegates to an agent (manager) whose interests diverge and whose actions are imperfectly observable, producing agency costs (monitoring costs, bonding costs, residual loss). Corporate governance — "the system by which companies are directed and controlled" (Cadbury Report, 1992) — is the architecture designed to minimize these costs and align managerial behavior with shareholder (and, on broader views, stakeholder) interests.

Governance mechanisms

Internal and external mechanisms work in concert. The board of directors is the primary internal monitor; debates center on board independence (the proportion of non-executive directors), CEO duality (whether one person is both CEO and chair), board diversity, and committee structure (audit, remuneration, nomination). Audit and disclosure mechanisms — external auditors, audit committees, and transparent financial reporting — combat the information asymmetry at the agency problem's core. Executive compensation attempts incentive alignment through equity, performance vesting, and clawbacks, though it can backfire by encouraging short-termism or excessive risk. External mechanisms include the market for corporate control (the disciplining threat of takeover), institutional-investor activism, credit and equity analysts, and the regulatory regime. The collapse of Enron and WorldCom prompted the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (2002), strengthening auditor independence, internal-control attestation, and executive accountability; the 2008 financial crisis spurred further reform of risk governance and pay.

It is worth noting a normative debate carried over from Lesson 7: a purely agency-theoretic, shareholder-primacy view of governance (Friedman, Jensen) competes with stakeholder-oriented governance, which expands the board's accountability to employees, communities, and the environment — reflected institutionally in codetermination (Germany) and the rise of ESG oversight.

Globalization: relativism, universalism, and ISCT

Global operations force the question of whose ethics govern. Ethical relativism asserts that moral standards are valid only relative to a culture; its business corollary is "when in Rome, do as the Romans do." While relativism rightly counsels humility and cultural sensitivity, in its strong form it is self-defeating: it would render all cross-cultural moral criticism illegitimate, including condemnation of slavery or apartheid, and it cannot adjudicate conflicts. Ethical universalism (or absolutism) holds that some standards bind everywhere — but naive universalism risks ethical imperialism, imposing one culture's norms as if they were universal.

Thomas Donaldson & Thomas Dunfee's (1994; 1999) Integrative Social Contracts Theory provides the leading reconciliation. ISCT distinguishes hypernorms — fundamental principles (e.g., respect for human dignity, basic rights, anti-corruption, procedural fairness) so basic they constrain all communities — from microsocial contracts, the legitimate, authentic norms communities generate within their moral free space. A local practice is ethical only if it stays within the bounds set by hypernorms. Donaldson (1996), in "Values in Tension," supplements this with practical tests: distinguish core human values from mere tradition, and ask whether a questionable practice is a genuine cultural difference or simply a violation dressed as one.

Corruption, bribery, and facilitation

A concrete and exam-relevant application is corruption. A bribe is a payment or inducement intended to corrupt a decision and secure improper advantage; a facilitation ("grease") payment is a small sum to expedite a routine, non-discretionary government action to which one is already entitled. The legal landscape diverges: the US Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (1977) contains a narrow facilitation-payment exception, whereas the UK Bribery Act (2010) prohibits them outright and imposes a corporate "failure to prevent" offense. Ethically, under ISCT, anti-corruption functions as a hypernorm: even where petty bribery is locally normalized, it violates a non-negotiable standard because it corrodes institutions, harms the poor disproportionately, and distorts markets (Transparency International data consistently link corruption to underdevelopment).

Globalization and supply-chain ethics

Extended global supply chains create the precise conditions — distance, diffusion, and low moral intensity (recall Jones, 1991, Lesson 6) — under which harms are overlooked. Disasters such as Rana Plaza (2013) crystallized debates over corporate responsibility for sub-suppliers, the limits of voluntary codes versus mandatory due-diligence laws (e.g., the EU and various national supply-chain acts), and living-wage and labor-rights obligations.

Contemporary frontier issues

  • AI and data ethics: algorithmic bias and fairness, explainability and accountability for automated decisions, the rights-vs-utility tension in deploying powerful systems, and labor displacement.
  • Privacy and surveillance: the conflict between data-driven value creation (and workplace monitoring) and the individual's right to privacy and autonomy — a direct application of rights theory (Lesson 3) against utilitarian efficiency.
  • Environmental and intergenerational ethics: climate change as the paradigm case of diffuse, deferred harm; duties to future generations; ESG accountability and the persistent problem of greenwashing.

Worked example

A multinational entering a new market is told that a six-figure "consulting fee" to a minister's relative is "how things are done." Relativist view: conform to local custom. Universalist/ISCT view: this is not a facilitation payment but a bribe corrupting a discretionary decision; anti-corruption is a hypernorm, so it is impermissible regardless of local prevalence and is also illegal under the FCPA/UK Bribery Act. Governance view: robust compliance, board-level oversight, and a speak-up channel should surface and block the payment. Decision: refuse, escalate, and reassess market entry strategy.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Equating good governance with compliance. Box-ticking on board structure can coexist with a toxic culture; substance (the actual independence and ethical tone) matters more than form.
  • Conflating bribes with facilitation payments to rationalize corruption.
  • Defaulting to relativism ("who are we to judge?") as a cover for convenient profit, or to imperialistic universalism that ignores legitimate cultural variation.
  • Treating ESG and AI ethics as PR rather than as genuine governance and risk obligations.

Synthesis: tying the course together

This lesson integrates the whole syllabus. The agency problem and bribery cases re-deploy the normative frameworks of Lessons 2-5 (utility, rights, virtue, justice); governance is the institutional answer to the decision-making failures of Lesson 6 (moral disengagement, weak motivation/character); and it operationalizes the accountability question raised by stakeholder theory in Lesson 7. The throughline of the course is that ethical business requires alignment at every level — individual character, organizational systems, and global institutions — anchored by universal respect for human dignity within a world of legitimate cultural diversity.

concepts.label: relativism vs universalism in global business
